core_intuitions: Patches-as-tokens: a 224x224 image is cut into a grid of 16x16 patches, each flattened and linearly projected into a D-dim vector, so an image becomes an ordinary token sequence a vanilla Transformer can eat.
The whole ViT forward pass is just BERT: [CLS | patch tokens] + learned positional embeddings, L Pre-LN Transformer blocks, then read the CLS row as the global image representation.

The CLIP contrastive objective builds an NxN image-text similarity matrix and trains the diagonal (matching pairs) to win a softmax over its whole row AND its whole column - the off-diagonal cells are the negatives, which is why huge batches matter.

SigLIP swaps CLIP's row/column softmax for an independent sigmoid on every cell of the NxN grid, so there is no cross-cell normalization to gather across GPUs - each pair is its own yes/no binary decision, which shards trivially and works at small batch sizes.
Learned positional embeddings lock a ViT to one resolution; to change resolution you reshape the 1D position table back into its 2D patch grid and bicubically resize it to the new patch count.

Plain ViTs dump global scratch information into a few random high-norm patch tokens, polluting attention maps with artifacts; adding a handful of dedicated register tokens gives that scratch space a home and cleans the spatial features up.

existing.name: vit-patchify
existing.issue: Renders correctly and reads clearly in both themes, but several glyphs fall back to missing-character tofu boxes: the arrow in 'Image [box] patches', the arrow in '16x16 patches [box] N=196', and a small box above '+ E_pos (learned)'. These were meant to be arrows/symbols and now look like rendering bugs.
existing.improvement: Replace the unicode arrow/symbol characters that render as tofu with either plain SVG arrow marks (a <path> or the same arrowhead style used elsewhere in the figure) or ASCII-safe wording (e.g. 'Image -> patches', 'N=196'). Verify no glyph falls back to a box in either theme after the change. Keep the rest of the layout untouched - it is a strong, clear figure.

existing.name: vit-arch-full-pipeline
existing.issue: Layout and content are excellent, but in DARK theme three of the rounded pass-through nodes ('prepend CLS token', 'add positional embeddings', 'CLS token output (row 0)') use a very dark navy fill with dark-grey text, giving near-invisible low contrast; the title 'ViT-B/16 Forward Pass' is also dim in dark mode.
existing.improvement: Make those rounded nodes theme-aware: in dark mode raise either the fill lightness or the text lightness so body text clears a comfortable contrast ratio (match the readable contrast of the lavender/tan boxes already in the figure). Brighten the title in dark mode too. Light theme already looks good - only the dark-theme token colors need fixing.

new_figures.name: clip-siglip-contrastive-matrix
new_figures.concept: The NxN image-text similarity matrix and how CLIP (row+column softmax) and SigLIP (independent per-cell sigmoid) differ in what they normalize over.
new_figures.rationale: CLIP and SigLIP are the two largest sections of the chapter and currently have zero figures. The single hardest thing to grasp from equations alone is that CLIP normalizes each cell against its entire row and column (a global coupling that forces huge batches and cross-GPU gathers), while SigLIP treats every cell as its own isolated binary decision (no coupling, shards trivially). A side-by-side matrix picture makes both the shared structure and the crucial difference click instantly.
new_figures.placement: Anchor at the start of the 'SigLIP: Replacing InfoNCE with Sigmoid Loss' section, just after the paragraph introducing the softmax communication bottleneck, so it retrospectively illuminates the CLIP loss too.
new_figures.brief: A single wide static infographic with two panels sharing one small NxN grid concept (use N=4 for legibility). LEFT PANEL titled 'CLIP: softmax over row + column'. Draw a 4x4 grid whose rows are labeled with 4 tiny image thumbnails (img1..img4, simple icon placeholders, NOT invented photos) down the left edge and whose columns are labeled with 4 short text captions (txt1..txt4) across the top. Each cell shows the cosine similarity S_ij; the 4 diagonal cells are visually marked as POSITIVE (a filled/checked accent) and off-diagonal cells as NEGATIVE (faint/hollow). Overlay a highlighted horizontal bracket spanning one full row AND a highlighted vertical bracket spanning one full column that both pass through the same diagonal cell, labeled 'softmax over the whole row' and 'softmax over the whole column' - the message: each positive must beat every other cell in its row and column, so the denominator sums across the entire batch. Add a small caption below: 'diagonal = matching pair (pull together); everything off-diagonal = negative (push apart); denominator couples the whole batch -> needs large N and a global gather'. RIGHT PANEL titled 'SigLIP: independent sigmoid per cell'. Draw the same 4x4 grid with the same diagonal-positive / off-diagonal-negative coloring, but instead of row/column brackets, draw a tiny independent sigmoid gate glyph inside EVERY cell (a small standalone circle/checkbox reading '+/-' or a mini sigma-curve), emphasizing that each cell is its own yes-this-pair-matches / no-it-doesnt binary decision with NO bracket linking cells. Add a small learnable-bias note 'b shifts the threshold (init negative: far more negatives than positives)'. Caption below: 'no cross-cell normalization -> each cell computed and summed independently -> shards across GPUs, works at small batch size'. Keep colors theme-safe (use accent/neutral roles, no hardcoded reds/greens; distinguish positive vs negative by fill density and a symbol as well as hue so it survives both themes and colorblind readers). Only illustrative integers (N=4); invent no similarity numbers - leave cells as 'S' or symbolic dots rather than fake decimals. Everything must fit the viewBox with clear left-to-right reading order and no overlapping labels.

new_figures.name: vit-register-tokens
new_figures.concept: Why plain ViT attention maps show high-norm artifact patches and how appended register tokens absorb that global scratch information to clean the spatial features.
new_figures.rationale: Register tokens are a distinctive, memorable, and genuinely non-obvious insight ('Vision Transformers Need Registers') that the text explains only in prose. The intuition - the model has no scratch space, so it hijacks a few random patch tokens to store global context, corrupting the attention map - is inherently visual: an artifact-speckled attention map versus a clean one, plus the sequence-level fix. A static before/after makes the whole idea land in one look.
new_figures.placement: Anchor inside the 'Register Tokens' subsection of DINOv2, right after the paragraph describing artifact tokens 'parking' global context in high-norm patches.
new_figures.brief: A static two-part infographic. TOP ROW: two side-by-side attention-map heatmaps over the same schematic image grid (a simple stylized object silhouette on a grid, NOT a real photo). LEFT map labeled 'Plain ViT': mostly the object region is warm/attended, but scatter 3-4 isolated bright hot cells in semantically empty background locations, labeled 'artifact tokens (anomalously high norm)'. RIGHT map labeled 'ViT + registers': the same object region attended cleanly and smoothly, background cool and uniform, no stray hot cells, labeled 'clean spatial features'. Make the difference obvious at a glance. BOTTOM ROW: the token-sequence view explaining the fix. Draw the input sequence as labeled boxes in order: one [CLS] box, then a run of 'patch tokens' boxes, then R small 'register' boxes (use R=4, distinct accent) appended at the end. A short annotation: 'registers get NO positional embedding; they are pure scratch space for global reasoning'. Then an arrow through 'Transformer blocks', after which show the output sequence with the register boxes greyed-out/crossed and a label 'registers discarded at output -> keep CLS + patch tokens'. A one-line caption tying it together: 'without a scratch slot the model parks global info in random patches (the artifacts); registers give that info a dedicated home, so patch attention stays semantic'. Theme-safe accent/neutral roles only; distinguish artifact hot cells by both brightness and an outline/marker so they read in light and dark; small illustrative integers only (R=4); no invented metrics. Ensure both heatmaps and the sequence row fit the viewBox with clear top-to-bottom reading order.

new_figures.name: pos-embed-interpolation
new_figures.concept: Bicubic interpolation of the learned positional-embedding grid when moving a ViT from its training resolution to a higher inference resolution.
new_figures.rationale: Resolution generalization is called out twice as important (a dedicated subsection plus an Interview Corner) and is a classic gotcha: the patch-embedding weights are fine, only the positional table is the wrong size. The fix - reshape the 1D position sequence back into its 2D patch grid and bicubically resize it - is far clearer as a picture of a small grid growing into a bigger grid than as reshape/permute code. Priority 3 because the code and interview answer already carry much of the load.
new_figures.placement: Anchor in the 'Positional Embeddings in ViT' section, right after the paragraph describing bicubic interpolation of the 14x14 grid to 24x24.
new_figures.brief: A static left-to-right pipeline showing the pos-embed resize. START: a 1D strip of position-embedding vectors labeled 'learned pos table, N=196 (+CLS)' with the CLS position drawn as a separate distinct cell split off to the side and labeled 'CLS pos - kept aside'. STEP 1 (reshape): an arrow 'reshape to 2D grid' leading to a 14x14 grid of small cells labeled '14 x 14 patch positions (train res 224)'. STEP 2 (interpolate): a bold arrow labeled 'bicubic interpolate' leading to a larger 24x24 (or 32x32) grid labeled 'resize to new patch count (e.g. 24 x 24 for 384px)'; visually convey smooth upsampling (draw the target grid finer/denser and, if helpful, faint diagonal 'sampling' guide lines between a source cell and the several target cells it feeds, to signal that new positions are interpolated, not copied). STEP 3 (flatten + re-attach): an arrow 'flatten + prepend CLS' back to a 1D strip labeled 'new pos table, N=576 (+CLS)', with the CLS cell rejoined at the front. Add a concise caption: 'patch-embedding weights are unchanged - they embed 14x14 patches at any resolution; only the positional table must be resized to the new number of patches'. Use theme-safe accent/neutral roles; keep grid cells uniform and legible; only illustrative integers (14, 24, 196, 576); no invented performance numbers. Ensure the largest grid and all labels fit inside the viewBox with a clean single reading path left to right.

summary: The chapter is well-served on its first half: the two existing figures (patchify, full ViT pipeline) cover 'patches-as-tokens' and 'ViT is just BERT' clearly - both KEEP with small REFINES (patchify has missing-glyph tofu boxes where arrows should be; the pipeline figure has dark-theme low-contrast on three navy nodes). The second half is figure-poor but concept-rich: CLIP and SigLIP, the two largest sections, have no figure at all, and their core intuition (the NxN similarity matrix; CLIP's row/column softmax coupling vs SigLIP's independent per-cell sigmoid) is exactly what a static infographic nails - this is the priority-1 new figure. Register tokens (priority 2) is a distinctive, memorable insight best shown as an artifact vs clean attention-map before/after plus the sequence-level fix. Positional-embedding interpolation (priority 3) is a recurring gotcha whose reshape-and-resize mechanic is clearer as a growing-grid picture than as code. All proposed figures are static - none require motion to convey the intuition.
